title: Cuban picadillo
subtitle: Spiced minced beef with olives, capers, and raisins
excerpt: Beef mince cooked in a Cuban sofrito of tomato, onion, pepper, and cumin, finished with green olives, capers, and raisins. The sweet-savoury-briny combination is distinctly Cuban, eaten with white rice.
sourceNotes: Cuban picadillo is a ground beef hash in a tomato-based sofrito sauce with olives, capers, and raisins. The sweet-sour-briny combination of raisins, olives, and capers is a Spanish colonial influence present across Latin American cuisines. In Cuba it is everyday food, eaten with white rice or used as a filling for pasteles, empanadas, and papas rellenas (stuffed potatoes). It is one of the simplest Cuban dishes to make and one of the most satisfying.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Reheat with a splash of water in a covered pan.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Excellent as a filling for baked potatoes, shepherd's pie base, or empanadas.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The finished picadillo keeps in the fridge for 4 days and reheats well.

content.text: Cuban picadillo has a flavour combination that sounds unlikely until you try it: ground beef with olives, capers, and raisins all in the same pan. The sweetness of the raisins, the brine of the olives and capers, the earthiness of cumin — these come together in a way that is rooted in Spanish colonial cooking and has been part of the Cuban pantry for centuries.

The technique is straightforward: sofrito first, then the beef, then the tomatoes, then the olives and raisins. The whole thing is done in 25 minutes. The raisins plump slightly in the tomato liquid and release their sweetness into the sauce. Leave them out if you prefer a more savoury dish, but the balance suffers.
Build the sofrito and cook the beef

Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{pepper-green}} green pepper and cook for 6 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Stir for 1 minute. Add the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince and break it up with a spoon. Cook for 6–8 minutes until browned through, with no pink remaining.

Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has reduced and thickened. Add the {{olives-green}} of olives, {{raisins}} of raisins (if using), and {{capers}} of capers (if using). Stir and simmer for 5 more minutes. Remove the bay leaf. Taste for seasoning.

items.cause: Too many raisins; very sweet tinned tomatoes
items.fix: Reduce raisins to 30 g or omit entirely. Add a splash of cider vinegar to balance the sweetness.
Rinse the olives and capers before adding to remove excess brine. Use less of each.
After browning, tilt the pan and spoon off excess fat before adding the tomatoes. Alternatively, drain the mince in a colander briefly.
Continue simmering uncovered. The liquid should reduce to a thick, chunky sauce that clings to the beef rather than pooling.

content.text: Cuban picadillo is weekday food: quick, satisfying, and flexible. In Cuba it is eaten with white rice and sometimes black beans, or used as a filling for fried empanadas and stuffed potatoes. The combination of olives, raisins, and capers is found across Latin American cuisines descended from Spanish colonial cooking — the same combination appears in Mexican picadillo and Puerto Rican sofrito dishes. In Cuba, this particular balance of sweet, salty, and savoury has been part of the table for generations.
